phases.label: Voter Enrolment
phases.description: Enrolment is compulsory for all eligible Australian citizens.
steps: Deadline: 7 days after the election writ is issued
voterActions: Ensure you are enrolled to avoid a fine
Update your address if you have moved

phases.label: Polling Day
phases.description: Compulsory voting for all enrolled citizens.
steps: Visit a polling place (usually schools or community halls)
Receive two ballot papers (Green for House, White for Senate)
voterActions: Vote at any polling place in your state/territory

eligibility.residence: Lived at your address for at least one month

glossary.definition: The legal requirement for all eligible Australian citizens to enrol and vote. Failure to vote without a valid reason results in a fine.
The system where voters rank candidates in order of preference (1, 2, 3...). Used for the House of Representatives.
The lower house of Parliament, consisting of 151 members, each representing one electoral division.
The upper house of Parliament, with 76 senators (12 from each state, 2 from each territory).
The independent federal agency responsible for conducting elections and maintaining the electoral roll.
A popular Australian tradition of buying a sausage in bread from a fundraising sizzle at a polling place on election day.
A geographical area represented by a member of the House of Representatives. Also known as a 'seat'.
A ballot paper that has not been filled out correctly (e.g., missing numbers or symbols) and cannot be counted.
A vote where a voter simply numbers the candidates in the order they appear on the ballot paper (1, 2, 3...). It is a valid vote.
Leaflets handed out by political party volunteers outside polling places suggesting how voters should rank their preferences.
More than 50% of the total formal votes. A candidate must reach this to be elected in the House of Representatives.
The result of an election after preferences are distributed between the two leading candidates (usually Labor and Coalition).
A constitutional procedure where both the House and the Senate are dissolved simultaneously to resolve a deadlock between the two houses.
The period from the issue of writs until the election result is clear, during which the government avoids making major policy decisions.
The AEC official responsible for the conduct of an election in a specific electoral division.
A vote cast by mail by a person who cannot attend a polling place on election day.
The option to vote in person before election day at designated early voting centres.
The paper on which a voter records their vote. Australians get two: Green (House) and White (Senate).

quickQuestions: What are the Green and White ballots for?

quiz.question: What happens if you are eligible but do not vote in an Australian federal election?
options: You are banned from the next election
quiz.explanation: Voting is compulsory in Australia. If you don't vote and don't have a valid reason, you may be issued a fine (currently $20).
quiz.funFact: Australia was one of the first countries in the world to introduce compulsory voting (in 1924).

quiz.question: How many members are in the Australian House of Representatives?
quiz.explanation: There are currently 151 members, each representing one electoral division.
quiz.funFact: The size of the House is approximately double the size of the Senate.

quiz.question: What is a 'Democracy Sausage'?
options: A sausage sizzle at a polling place
quiz.explanation: It's a cultural tradition in Australia where community groups hold sausage sizzles at polling places to raise funds.
quiz.funFact: In 2016, 'Democracy Sausage' was named the Australian Word of the Year.

quiz.question: What is 'Preferential Voting'?
options: Voters pick only their favorite candidate
Voters rank candidates in order of preference
The candidate with the most '1' votes wins automatically
quiz.explanation: Voters must number candidates 1, 2, 3... to show their order of preference. If no one gets 50%+, lower candidates are eliminated and votes redistributed.
quiz.funFact: Preferential voting ensures that the elected member is supported by a majority of voters in their division.

quiz.question: How many Senators does each state have in Australia?
quiz.explanation: Each of the six states has 12 senators. The two territories (ACT and NT) have 2 each.
quiz.funFact: Senators serve 6-year terms, but elections are usually held for half of the Senate every 3 years.

quiz.question: What is a 'Double Dissolution' election?
options: When two parties merge before an election
When both the House and the Senate are dissolved to resolve a deadlock
When a voter casts two ballots by mistake
When an election is held twice in one year
quiz.explanation: Triggered under Section 57 of the Constitution when the Senate twice rejects a bill passed by the House. All seats in both houses are contested.
quiz.funFact: There have been only seven double dissolution elections in Australian history, the most recent in 2016.
